title: Ranunculus subglaber
taxonomy: Ranunculus subglaber is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. The Ranunculus subglaber is native to the Mediterranean region and parts of Europe. It is a member of the Ranunculus genus, which consists of over 600 species of buttercups and related plants. The classification of Ranunculus subglaber is further divided into subcategories, including its kingdom (Plantae) and order (Ranunculales). This classification helps to identify and categorize the unique characteristics of the Ranunculus subglaber.

preparations: Ranunculus subglaber is not commonly associated with herbal preparations due to its toxicity. side_effects: Ranunculus subglaber is a plant that contains toxic compounds, including protoanemonin and ranunculin. These compounds can cause skin irritation, blistering, and pain if the plant comes into contact with the skin. Ingestion of the plant can lead to nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The toxins in Ranunculus subglaber can also cause kidney and liver damage if ingested in large quantities. Prolonged exposure to the plant's toxic compounds can lead to more severe health issues.
intro: Ranunculus subglaber, also known as the lesser caltha or lesser marsh-marigold, is a perennial plant species belonging to the buttercup family (Ranunculaceae). The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which may aid in wound healing and the treatment of skin conditions such as eczema and acne. The bioactive constituents of Ranunculus subglaber include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. Traditionally, the plant is used in herbal preparations such as infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat skin conditions and promote wound healing.
